title: Future Changes in Snowpack, Snowmelt, and Runoff Potential Extremes Over North America
authors: Cho, E., McCrary, R. R., & Jacobs, J. M.
abstract: This paper examines future changes in extreme snow water equivalent, snowmelt, and runoff potential over North America using NA-CORDEX regional climate model simulations.
keywords: snowpack, snowmelt, runoff potential, climate change, NA-CORDEX, North America

title: Extreme Value Snow Water Equivalent and Snowmelt for Infrastructure Design Over the Contiguous United States
abstract: This paper develops 25- and 100-year return level design maps of snow water equivalent and snowmelt over the contiguous United States and compares them with NOAA Atlas 14 precipitation-frequency estimates.
keywords: SWE, snowmelt, infrastructure design, extreme value analysis, NOAA Atlas 14, rain-on-snow
local_path: papers/Water Resources Research - 2020 - Cho - Extreme Value Snow Water Equivalent and Snowmelt for Infrastructure Design Over the.pdf

title: Global Snow Seasonality Regimes from Satellite Records of Snow Cover
authors: Johnston, J., Jacobs, J. M., & Cho, E.
abstract: This paper develops globally applicable snow seasonality classes using satellite-based snow cover records, MODIS data, machine learning, and decision tree rules.
keywords: snow seasonality, MODIS, satellite, machine learning, snow cover, global

title: Extending the Utility of Space-Borne Snow Water Equivalent Observations Over Vegetated Areas with Data Assimilation
authors: Pflug, J. M., Wrzesien, M. L., Kumar, S. V., Cho, E., Arsenault, K. R., Houser, P. R., & Vuyovich, C. M.
abstract: This paper investigates how synthetic space-borne snow water equivalent observations can improve snowpack estimates through data assimilation using an observing system simulation experiment.
keywords: SWE, SAR, data assimilation, forest canopy, remote sensing, OSSE, Noah-MP
